1.1: Habakkuk's dialogue with YHWH (ca. 605-598 BCE) is the OT's most direct expression of prophetic complaint about divine justice: Why do you tolerate wrong? (1:3); Why are you silent while the wicked swallow up the more righteous? (1:13). YHWH's answer — he is raising up the Babylonians as the instrument of judgment — only deepens the question: how can a holy God use a more wicked nation to judge a less wicked one? The book's resolution is the three-chapter arc of complaint → divine response → prophetic praise (ch. 3): the righteous will live by faith in YHWH's promises even when the present situation appears to contradict those promises. The book models the theodicy of faith: not a logical resolution of the problem of evil, but a trust in the character of YHWH that sustains through crisis.
1.2: How long, O LORD, must I call for help and you will not hear? — ad-anah YHWH shiwwati: the ad-anah (how long?) formula opens the classical lament structure (Ps 13:1-2; 74:10; 89:46; 94:3). Habakkuk deploys the formal complaint (rib) genre: a covenant party accusing the covenant-keeper of failing to uphold treaty obligations. Chamas (violence) is the keyword of the book and the specific trigger-word for the Flood in Gen 6:11-13 ('the earth was filled with chamas'). The implicit argument: if chamas required divine intervention then (the Flood), why does YHWH tolerate it now?
1.3: Why do you make me see injustice and stare at wickedness? — the visual compulsion (tabbitu = stare intently) portrays the prophet as forced witness to evil against his will. Shod (destruction) and chamas (violence) alliterate in Hebrew; the sound-reinforcement underscores the accusation. Rib (legal dispute) and madon (contention) frame the social disorder as a collapsed judicial system — not merely violence but the failure of covenant mechanisms designed to address violence. The prophet's suffering as witness is itself a form of injustice: YHWH makes him see what should not be tolerated.
1.4: The law is paralyzed and justice never prevails — watafug ha-Torah: pug (become numb, paralyzed, grow cold/stiff) describes the Torah as covenant instrument rendered unresponsive. Mishpat lo yetze la-netzach (justice never prevails permanently) — netzach = 'forever' or 'to a decisive end.' Mequqqal ha-mishpat (justice comes out twisted/bent) — qalal = distortion, the opposite of the straight judicial measure. Judicial corruption is the covenant's internal failure: more corrosive than external enemies because it destroys the community's self-repair mechanisms.
1.5: Look out among the nations and pay attention; be utterly astonished — YHWH's first answer shifts the prophetic gaze from Judean local injustice to the international stage. Himmahu (be utterly astonished) — the root tamah = astonishment at the incomprehensible. Ki po'el po'al bimechem (I am doing something in your own days) — the divine work is already in motion within the prophet's lifetime. Paul cites this verse in Acts 13:41 at Pisidian Antioch as a warning against rejecting the gospel: the same incomprehensibility-structure applies — the new covenant work is as unbelievable as the Babylonian judgment, and equally dangerous to dismiss.
1.6: I am raising up the Chaldeans — hineni meqim et-ha-Kasdim: the Chaldeans = the neo-Babylonian empire under Nabopolassar and Nebuchadnezzar II, who defeated Assyria at Nineveh (612 BCE) and Egypt at Carchemish (605 BCE). The Chaldeans' military characteristics described in vv6b-11 are historically accurate: extreme speed of advance, comprehensive siege capability, cavalry superiority. YHWH's 'hineni raising up' frames Babylon as an instrument, not an autonomous actor — the same framing as Isa 10:5 ('Woe to Assyria, the rod of my anger').
1.7: They answer to no authority but their own — mimmennu mishpato use'eto yetze: their judicial norm (mishpat) and their elevation (se'et) proceed from themselves alone. This is the theological danger: YHWH deploys a power that acknowledges no authority above itself, not even YHWH's. The Babylonian empire operated with an absolute divine-mandate ideology — Nebuchadnezzar as earthly regent of Marduk, accountable to no external standard. The instrument that forgets it is an instrument is also what Isa 10:12-15 warns against in the Assyrian parallel.
1.8: Their horses are faster than leopards and fiercer than wolves at dusk — ze'evei erev (wolves of evening) = wolves that have been hungry all day, hunting at their most intense. The animal metaphors (leopard, wolf, eagle) were standard ANE military comparisons; Babylonian cavalry was genuinely feared for speed and surprise. Uts (they swoop) = the eagle-attack image for sudden assault from distance. Babylonian annals and the books of Kings both describe the rapid advance of Nebuchadnezzar's forces across Judah — from the north, down through the hill country, to Jerusalem — in a matter of weeks.
1.9: Their faces press forward like the east wind — megammat penehem qadimah: the ruach qadim (east wind, sirocco) was associated in the ANE with destruction and death — Gen 41:6 (scorching wind), Ezek 17:10 (the east wind that dries up the transplanted vine), Jon 4:8 (the scorching east wind). The Babylonians came geographically from the northeast but the directional force of their advance felt like the destroying desert wind. Yeasef kakkhol shevi (he scoops up captives like sand) — the mass-deportation policy confirmed by Babylonian administrative records of Judahite exiles.
1.10: They mock kings and scoff at rulers; they laugh at every fortress — the Babylonian attitude to existing political structures was contemptuous; they absorbed kingdoms rapidly. Yitsbor afar wayilkedeha (they pile up earth/siege ramps and take it) — the systematic Babylonian siege methodology: earthworks, ramps, battering rams. Excavation at Lachish (destroyed ca. 701 BCE by Assyria, again ca. 597 BCE by Babylon) shows the characteristic ramp-and-assault technique. No fortress was genuinely impregnable against a determined Babylonian siege — as Jerusalem discovered in 586 BCE.
1.11: Then his spirit charges ahead; he sweeps through and transgresses, ascribing his might to his own god — az chalaf ruach wayya'avor: the Babylonian momentum carries forward past any resistance. Veashem zu kocho le'elohav (ascribing his might to his own god) — Babylon attributes military success to Marduk, not to YHWH who is the actual agent deploying them. This is the same pattern as Isa 10:12-15: Assyria's arrogance in claiming 'by the strength of my hand I have done this.' The instrument forgets it is an instrument — a pattern that sets up Babylon's own eventual judgment (Rev 18).
1.12: Are you not from everlasting, O LORD my God, my Holy One? — the prophet pivots from complaint to theological confession as the ground for his second complaint. Miqqedem (from everlasting) — YHWH's eternal nature is the framework within which the historical anomaly of Babylon must be interpreted. Lo namut (we will not die) — covenant confidence, not personal optimism; the people of the eternal God cannot be finally destroyed. Shattam le-mishpat (you have appointed them for judgment, for correction) — the prophet accepts YHWH's sovereignty even while questioning his method; this acceptance is itself a form of emunah.
1.13: Your eyes are too pure to approve of evil; you cannot look upon wickedness — tehorah einayim mire'ot ra': the divine holiness is not merely the absence of evil but an active intolerance of it. This is the sharpest formulation of the theodicy problem: YHWH's holiness means he cannot countenance evil (lo tuchal habbit el-amal) — yet he is apparently using a more wicked nation to punish a less wicked one. The ANE understanding of divine justice was retributive proportionality; Habakkuk applies that standard to YHWH's method and finds the apparent inconsistency. The book does not resolve this by argument but by the theophany of ch3.
1.14: Why have you made humanity like the fish of the sea — the fishing metaphor dominating vv14-17 was a powerful ANE image for imperial conquest: the great empire as fisherman, the nations as helpless catch. Egyptian and Assyrian reliefs depict conquered peoples as fish drawn up in nets. Lo moshel bo (no one to rule over them) — fish and creeping things lack the hierarchical ordering that would give them defense; they are atomized, anarchic prey. The absence of governance is both a description of Judean social collapse (v4) and of the helplessness before Babylonian power.
1.15: He hauls all of them up with a fishhook; he drags them away in his net — the sequence: individual fishhook → net → dragnet portrays systematic, comprehensive harvest of peoples. The escalation from individual to mass-catch mirrors Babylon's policy: first individual targets (kings, nobles), then groups (craftsmen, soldiers), then whole populations. The Babylonian chronicles and 2 Kings 24-25 document exactly this progression in the Judahite campaigns of 605, 598-597, and 586 BCE.
1.16: So he offers sacrifices to his net and burns incense to his dragnet — the military instrument becomes the object of worship: the idolatry of power, in which military success validates itself as divine favor. Ki vahem shamen chelqo (for by them his portion is rich) — the economic theology of empire: prosperity = divine approval; the mechanism of domination is sacralized. The NT parallel is Paul's critique of those whose 'god is their belly' (Phil 3:19) — the proximate cause of success becomes the ultimate object of devotion.
1.17: Will he keep on emptying his net, slaying nations without pity, forever? — the final complaint question stands without immediate answer; the reader enters ch2 still holding the tension. Hahiqreq yariqqo (will he keep emptying his net?) — the insatiable appetite of empire, the net perpetually refilled. Velokhmal laharog goyim (slaying nations without pity, without sparing) — the covenant accusation-formula for merciless genocide; the same phrase Obadiah uses against Edom (Obad 14: 'you should not have handed over the survivors'). The question stands as the book's central tension.

2.1: I will take my post at the watchtower and station myself on the rampart — al mishmarti e'emad: the mishmar (watch-post, guard station) was the elevated position from which the prophetic watchman received and proclaimed divine messages. Ezekiel 33:7 develops the watchman metaphor most fully: 'I have made you a watchman for the house of Israel.' The elevated position implies wider perspective: the prophet sees the divine movements hidden from ground-level. Va'atsapeh lirot mah yadabber-bi (I will watch to see what he will say to me) — divine communication as deliberate waiting posture; the prophet positions himself to receive.
2.2: Write down the vision; inscribe it plainly on tablets so that the one who reads it can carry the message — kotov chazon uvaar al-ha-lukhot: tablet-inscription for public display; baer (make plain, explain) appears in Deut 1:5 where Moses 'explains' the Torah. Written prophecy preserves the oracle for the future generation that will see its fulfillment. The parallel with Moses writing on tablets connects Habakkuk's vision to the Sinai covenant documentation, suggesting the same level of divine authority and public accountability. Lema'an yarutz qore vo (so that a runner reading it can carry the message) — legible at speed; urgent, public proclamation.
2.3: For the vision awaits its appointed time; it speaks of the final outcome and will not prove false — ki od chazon lammo'ed: mo'ed (appointed time, set season) is the eschatological vocabulary of the covenant calendar — the same word for the sacred assembly seasons (Lev 23). The vision of the qetz (final outcome/end) is temporally fixed in YHWH's sovereign schedule. Im yahemeh chakeh lo (though it lingers, wait for it) — the imperative of patient waiting (chakeh) is the practical application of the 'appointed time' theology; cf. 'when the fullness of time had come' (Gal 4:4).
2.4: The righteous shall live by his faithfulness — vetzaddiq beemunato yichyeh: emunah (faithfulness, steadfastness, reliability) covers a semantic range from 'reliability/fidelity' to 'trust/belief.' The contrast with the puffed-up soul (whose way is not right) establishes emunah as the opposite of self-reliant arrogance: the righteous person is characterized by covenant-faithfulness that endures under pressure. The DSS commentary (1QpHab, 2nd-1st c. BCE) reads 'his faith' as faithfulness to the Teacher of Righteousness. Paul's three NT citations (Rom 1:17; Gal 3:11; Heb 10:38) drove the Reformation's doctrine of justification by faith and remain the most-cited Minor Prophet verse in Christian theology.
2.5: Wine is a deceiver; the proud man cannot stay put — vehakkayin boged: wine as the metaphor for Babylon's hubris and insatiability; the intoxication of imperial power. He'hir nefesh ketze'ol (he opens his appetite as wide as Sheol) — the Babylonian appetite for conquest is insatiable as death itself. Isa 5:14 uses the same Sheol-image for Jerusalem's arrogance. The comparison with Sheol/Death places imperial power in the category of cosmic anti-creation forces — the forces that must ultimately yield to the Creator who defined death's boundaries.
2.6: Woe to the one who piles up wealth that is not his own — the first of five woe oracles (vv6-20) structuring Babylon's judgment. The ANE lament-form (hoy woe-cry) was spoken over corpses; to address Babylon with hoy places it in the position of the dead, anticipating the verdict before its execution. Hamakbir lo she'eino (who piles up what is not his) — the debt-accumulation through imperial predation. Avitot (pledged goods) taken as security and never returned violates Torah debt-law (Exod 22:26: 'if you take your neighbor's cloak in pledge, return it by sunset'); the imperial scale of this violation is the Mosaic law written in blood.
2.7: Will not your debtors suddenly turn on you? — the reversal of creditor-debtor relationships; those Babylon has victimized become the agents of its downfall. Uvezu lekha (and you will become their plunder) — the precise symmetry of the covenant curse: what Babylon took (baz, plunder) will be taken from Babylon. Historical fulfillment came rapidly: Persia conquered Babylon in 539 BCE; Cyrus reversed the deportation policies and issued the decree allowing Judahite return (Ezra 1). The empire built on unjust profit was itself plundered.
2.8: Because you have plundered many nations, all the surviving peoples will plunder you — the lex talionis at imperial scale: the covenant's retributive-justice principle applied internationally. Dam adam uchamas eretz (for the blood shed against humanity and for the violence against the land) — the four-term indictment: blood of individuals, violence against the land-ecosystem, the city as communal structure, and inhabitants as persons. This comprehensive scope anticipates Revelation's indictment of Babylon: 'in you was found the blood of prophets and of saints, and of all who have been slain on earth' (Rev 18:24).
2.9: Woe to him who makes unjust profit for his household, to place his nest on high — second woe: against the economics of security-through-exploitation. Lebatzo batzah ra' (to make unjust profit) — betza (gain by cutting off, unjust profit) is the term for profit extracted by force or fraud. Sum bammaron qinno (to place his nest on high) — the bird-nest-as-security metaphor; cf. Obad 4 (Edom's eagle-nest arrogance). The height built on exploitation of others is the inversion of the covenant principle: security comes from YHWH (maoz in Nah 1:7), not from the accumulated plunder of others.
2.10: You have plotted the disgrace of your own household — yatzta boshet leveitekha: the ironic reversal; the strategy designed to secure the household has produced its shame. Boshet (public shame, disgrace) is the community-visible opposite of the kavod (honor/glory) that motivated the unjust accumulation. Vechotet nafshekhah (you have brought guilt/sin upon yourself) — the act of exploitation is simultaneously self-condemnation: the legal guilt adheres to the perpetrator. The self-defeating logic of covenant violation: the means of security become the means of destruction.
2.11: For the very stone in the wall will cry out against you, and the wooden beam will echo the charge — aven miqqir tizaq: the building materials themselves become legal witnesses. Stones from plundered cities or buildings constructed with unjust materials carry the testimony of their origin. The entire built environment of the imperial house is a witness against it. Jesus draws on this Habakkuk image in Luke 19:40 ('if they keep silent, the stones will cry out') — the created order as witness to both injustice and the presence of the covenant King.
2.12: Woe to the one who builds a city on bloodshed and founds a town by injustice — third woe: against imperial city-building. The ancient Near Eastern city was the symbol of civilization and divine blessing — founded by gods, protected by patron deities, the seat of justice. Damim (bloodshed) and avlah (injustice) as building materials invert the civic ideal. Nebuchadnezzar's building inscriptions boast his construction projects; Habakkuk indicts their foundations. Jer 51:58 applies nearly identical language to Babylon's walls and gates: 'the thick wall of Babylon shall be leveled to the ground.'
2.13: Is it not indeed from the LORD of hosts that peoples labor only to fuel the flames — halo hineh me'et YHWH tzvaot: YHWH is the agent who ensures empire's labor ultimately comes to nothing. Vile'umim beyede-aish yig'au (nations exhaust themselves for nothing) — the tohu (vanity/futility) of Eccl 1 applied to imperial labor. The futility of empire-building apart from YHWH is covenant logic: systems built on exploitation are self-consuming. Jer 51:58 repeats this word-for-word about Babylon's actual walls, prophesying their collapse.
2.14: For the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD, as the waters cover the sea — the great eschatological counter-reality at the center of the five woe oracles. This verse is nearly verbatim with Isa 11:9 ('the earth will be full of the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea') — sharing a common eschatological tradition. Yeda'at kevod-YHWH (knowledge of YHWH's glory) is the full covenant-relationship knowledge of YHWH's kabod. The empire covers the earth with violence now; the kavod will cover it ultimately, completely, and permanently. This verse is the answer to the theodicy-question of 1:13: YHWH cannot countenance evil forever because the final state is his glory filling the earth.
2.15: Woe to the one who makes his neighbor drink — fourth woe: against forcing the cup of violence on others. Hamashqeh re'ehu chemato (who gives his neighbor to drink from his cup of wrath) — the cup-of-wrath metaphor (Jer 25:15-29; Isa 51:17-23; Rev 14:10) represents forced experience of divine/imperial punishment. Ushmach (so that you may exult/gloat) — the sadism of power, not merely strategic domination but enjoyment of the victim's degradation. The ANE treaty-curse literature includes curses about forced humiliation; Habakkuk is applying this category to Babylon's conduct toward the nations.
2.16: You will be glutted with shame instead of glory — shaba'ta qalon mikavodʼ: the exact inversion of desired outcome; the pursuit of imperial kavod produces its opposite. Shovvah gam atta (drink in turn) — the cup of wrath passes; Babylon which administered judgment-by-cup will drink from it. Kos yamin YHWH (the cup in YHWH's right hand) — the right hand = the hand of covenantal power and judicial authority; YHWH's cup overturns Babylon's pretended glory. Historical fulfillment: Persia's conquest, Babylon's dismantling, Cyrus's decree — the empire's glory became its shame.
2.17: For the violence you did to Lebanon will overwhelm you — ki chamas Levanon yekhasekha: the Lebanon cedar forests were systematically stripped by successive empires (Assyria, Babylon) for timber. The ecological devastation is cognizable as a covenant crime alongside human atrocities. Washod behemot yechittan (the terror brought upon the wildlife will recoil on you) — the covenant's scope extends to the non-human world; compare Gen 9:5 where YHWH demands an accounting for animal blood. The land and its creatures are covenant parties, not merely objects; their destruction falls under the same retributive logic as human violence.
2.18: What use is a carved idol when its maker has shaped it? — fifth woe: against idolatry, the theological root of all preceding covenant violations. Mah hoil pesel ki fesalhu — the idol is human handiwork; its uselessness is self-evident. The idol-polemic tradition (Isa 40:18-20; 44:9-20; Jer 10:1-16; Ps 115:4-8) exposes the absurdity of worshipping what you made. Yoreih sheker (a teacher of lies) — the idol actively deceives; it trains its worshipers in falsehood about reality. This is not merely irreligion but epistemological corruption: the idolater has no reliable account of the world.
2.19: Woe to the one who says to a wooden idol, 'Awake!' — the fifth woe made explicit; addressing the idol in the 2nd person intensifies the absurdity. Hu' yoreh (can it really teach?) — the rhetorical question whose answer is obviously no. Hineh hu' tafus zahav vakesef (see — it is overlaid with gold and silver) — external appearance of wealth and beauty cannot supply what is absent internally: vekhol-ruach ein beqirbo (there is no breath at all within it). The contrast positions v20: the idol has no ruach; YHWH has his temple. The idolater has gold but not life; the covenant community has the living God in his dwelling.
2.20: But the LORD is in his holy temple; let all the earth be silent before him — vaADONAI behekhal qodsho has milfanav kol-ha-aretz: the majestic conclusion to the five woe oracles. Against the silent idols (v19) stands YHWH who is present in his temple (whether the cosmic temple of creation or the Jerusalem sanctuary). Has (silence, hush!) — the exclamatory command for reverential silence before the divine sovereign; cf. Zeph 1:7; Amos 6:10; Zech 2:13. The earth's silence before YHWH is the counterpoint to the clamor of empire and the empty words of idols. This verse is the theological resolution of chs 1-2's crisis: YHWH has not vacated the field; he is enthroned, and the nations must be silent before him.

3.1: A prayer of Habakkuk the prophet, set to Shigionoth — shigionoth (plural) may indicate a dithyrambic, passionately irregular song form; the singular shiggayon appears in Ps 7's superscription. Ch3 is formally a psalm — with Selah pause markers at vv3, 9, 13 and a liturgical instruction at v19 ('for the choir director: on stringed instruments') — appended to the prophetic dialogue. The formal shift from dialogic oracle to liturgical hymn marks the book's resolution: the theodicy of faith culminates not in argument but in worship. Habakkuk enters the argument through the mouth of the complaint-prophet (chs 1-2) and exits through the mouth of the praise-psalmist (ch3).
3.2: O LORD, I have heard your report and stood in awe; in the midst of the years, revive your work — shim'akha (your report, what I have heard of you) = the content of chs 1-2 now received; the hearing generates yare'ti (reverential awe). Chayyeh po'alkha bekerev shanim (revive your work in the midst of the years) — the Exodus-conquest theophany (described in vv3-15) is the template: the prophet prays for that divine pattern to recur in history. Beshinah rachem (in wrath remember mercy) — not a cancellation of wrath but its tempering by covenant chesed; the same dynamic as Exod 34:6-7 (YHWH merciful and slow to anger, yet not clearing the guilty). This phrase became the primary Habakkuk text in Jewish liturgy for the Days of Awe (Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur).
3.3: God came from Teman; the Holy One came from Mount Paran — the theophany locates YHWH approaching from the south/southeast: Teman = Edomite territory; Paran = the Sinai wilderness. This is the traditional direction of the Exodus theophany: Deut 33:2 ('the LORD came from Sinai, rose from Seir, shone forth from Mount Paran'); Judg 5:4-5 (Deborah's Song: 'when you marched from the field of Edom, the earth trembled'). The cosmic Warrior-God tradition in which YHWH advances from his Sinai base is one of the oldest forms in Israelite poetry — pre-monarchic, deeply embedded in the wilderness period's memory. Selah invites pause on this primordial approach.
3.4: His radiance shone like light; rays of brightness came from his hand; and there was the hiding place of his power — qarnayim miyado lo: the qarnayim (rays/horns of light) is the same root as Exod 34:29-30, where Moses's face 'sent out rays' (qaran) after encountering YHWH. The divine radiance that made Moses's face shine and terrified Israel at Sinai is here the universal light of the cosmic theophany. Vesham chebyonezo (and there was the hiding place of his power) — the paradox: the most visible aspect of YHWH (the light) conceals his deepest power; the brightness is veil as much as disclosure.
3.5: Pestilence marched before him, and burning plague went out at his feet — dever (pestilence/plague) and reshef (burning plague, pestilence-fire) are the divine warrior's heralds. In Canaanite mythology Reshef was the god of plague and war; here demythologized into YHWH's attendants. The Exodus plagues (Exod 7-12) are the historical referent: pestilence was the covenant instrument against Egypt. YHWH's cosmic march in ch3 recapitulates the Exodus on a universal scale — the plagues that struck Egypt are now the standard advance of the divine army.
3.6: He stopped and shook the earth; with a look he scattered the nations; the ancient mountains crumbled — amad vayemoded eretz (he stood and measured/shook the earth): madad can mean both 'to measure' (surveying) and 'to shake.' Vayater goyim (he scattered the nations) — cf. Ps 68:1 ('let God arise, let his enemies be scattered'). Harerei ad (ancient mountains) and givot olam (eternal hills) — the most stable features of the physical world become unstable at the divine approach. The geological permanence that ANE peoples associated with divine foundations (mountains were the seats of gods) yields before the Creator who established them.
3.7: I saw the tents of Cushan in distress; the tent-curtains of the land of Midian trembled — Cushan (likely south Arabian or Nubian) and Midian (south of Judah, east of Sinai) represent the surrounding nations who witness and tremble at the theophany. Their trembling at a God who is not their own demonstrates YHWH's universal sovereignty — not merely a regional deity. Midian appears in the Exodus tradition as the region of Moses's calling (Exod 3) and the initial Sinai theophanies; their trembling confirms this ch3 theophany recapitulates the Exodus event.
3.8: Were you angry at the rivers, O LORD? Was your wrath against the rivers, or your fury against the sea? — the rhetorical questions invoke the Exodus traditions: the Reed Sea crossing (Exod 14-15), the Jordan crossing (Josh 3-4). The waters/sea (yam) in the OT cosmic framework represent both literal waters and the primordial chaos (Gen 1:2; Ps 89:9-10). YHWH's action against the waters was not anger at the waters per se but defense of his people — the purpose clause follows immediately. Tirkav al-susekha markevotekha yeshu'ah (you mounted your horses, your chariots of salvation) — YHWH as divine charioteer, the ANE divine-warrior image at its most concentrated.
3.9: Your bow was unsheathed and made bare; your sworn oaths to the tribes stood firm — shevu'ot mattot omer selah: 'your sworn oaths (shevu'ot) to the tribes (mattot)' — the covenant oaths to the patriarchal tribes (Gen 15; 22:16-18; 26:3; 28:13-15) are the theological ground for the Exodus-conquest. YHWH's military action on Israel's behalf is covenant-oath fulfillment, not arbitrary favor. Tivqa aretz neharot (you split the earth open with rivers) — the water-splitting power now turns from sea-parting (Exod 14) to river-opening; the same sovereign who controlled the Reed Sea controls all water systems.
3.10: The mountains saw you and convulsed; the floodwaters swept past; the deep roared out its voice — ra'ukha yachilu harim (the mountains writhed in labor-pain/fear) — the birth-agony metaphor for mountains suggests cosmic upheaval reaching to creation's foundations. Tehom natan qolo (the deep [tehom] gave its voice) — the primordial deep of Gen 1:2 that YHWH subdued at creation speaks again in response to the theophany; the same force that threatened chaos acknowledges the Creator's passage. The cosmic acknowledgment confirms that the divine approach is not merely natural phenomena but the Creator exercising original creative authority.
3.11: The sun and moon stood still in their places at the gleam of your flying arrows — shemesh yareach amad zevulah: the primary allusion is Josh 10:12-13 (Joshua's long day at Gibeon: 'Sun, stand still at Gibeon, and moon, in the Valley of Aijalon'). The astronomical bodies halted as light from YHWH's own weapons replaced solar illumination. The theophany in Habakkuk recapitulates the conquest battles where cosmic forces were enlisted in YHWH's war — confirming that the coming divine action against Babylon will be as interventionist as the original conquest of Canaan.
3.12: In your fury you strode through the earth; in your anger you trampled the nations — bezan titz'ad eretz bezaaf tashresh goyim: the divine march through the earth is simultaneously a judgment-march. The nations underfoot in the theophany are the historical enemies of Israel (Egypt, Canaan) whose defeat the theophany recapitulates — and by implication Babylon, whose defeat the prophet anticipates. The scope (earth, nations) contrasts with the specific purpose in v13 (your people, your anointed one): cosmic action for covenant-particular salvation.
3.13: You went out to save your people, to save your anointed one — yatza'ta leshaya' ammekha leshaya' et-meshichekha: the entire cosmic theophany of vv3-12 has one purpose: salvation of the covenant people and the anointed one. Meshichekha (your anointed one) may refer to Israel as the corporate anointed, to the Davidic king, or proleptically to the messianic figure. Machatzta rosh mibbeit rasha' (you struck the head from the wicked household) — decapitation imagery for the empire (Egypt, Canaan, Babylon) whose power is broken at the top. The 'wicked household' whose head is removed is the specific counterpart to 'your people' who are saved.
3.14: You pierced the head of his warlords with their own weapons — naqavta vematav rosh: the ironic judgment pattern in which the enemy's own weapons become the instrument of destruction — the same irony as Haman executed on Mordecai's gallows (Esth 7:10), David beheading Goliath with the giant's own sword (1 Sam 17:51). Yelaku liseroteri la'akol anavim bamister (they charged to scatter us, exulting as if to devour the poor in secret) — the oppressor's confidence in secrecy and surprise is exposed; the cosmic warrior sees and acts against what the empire thought it could do unobserved.
3.15: You trod through the sea with your horses, churning through great mounds of water — the final theophany image returns to the sea: darekhta vayam susekha (you trod with your horses through the sea). YHWH did not merely part the water at the Reed Sea but charged through it with his cavalry — the active-warrior image contrasting with the passive 'waters parted' narrative. Chomer mayim rabbim (great mounds/heap of water) — the walls of water at the Reed Sea parting (Exod 14:22: 'the waters were a wall to them on their right and left'). The Exodus event is the anchor for all the theophany imagery in ch3.
3.16: When I heard, my whole body trembled; at the sound my lips quivered; rottenness crept into my bones — the prophet's somatic response to the theophany; the physical body registers what the intellect has received. Veteqev yavi' batzamai (rottenness entered my bones) — complete dissolution of bodily confidence; cf. Dan 10:8 (Daniel's strength left him at the vision), Ezek 3:14-15 (Ezekiel overwhelmed). Asher anuach leyom tzarah (that I must wait quietly for the day of trouble) — the resolution: waiting posture (nuach, rest/quiet) for the coming Babylonian invasion, now understood as within YHWH's sovereign plan. This is the emunah of 2:4 expressed as embodied waiting-posture under physical terror.
3.17: Though the fig tree does not blossom and there is no fruit on the vine; though the olive harvest fails and the fields produce no food — the most comprehensive statement of covenantal faith in the OT. Habakkuk lists every element of the ANE subsistence economy: te'enah (fig tree), gefen (vine), ma'aseh zayt (olive yield), sadot (fields, grain), miqneh (livestock), baqar (cattle). The six-term list covers every agricultural and pastoral element of a pre-industrial economy. Total failure of all six simultaneously = worst imaginable catastrophe; the systematic 'though... though... though' accumulation escalates to the point of total deprivation. This is a description of what the Babylonian conquest and subsequent famine would produce — lived experience that threatened to make faith appear absurd.
3.18: Yet I will exult in the LORD; I will rejoice in the God of my salvation — va'ani baADONAI e'eloze agil be'elohei yish'i: the pivot on va'ani (yet I/but I) is the great faith-declaration of the book. Two verbs of rejoicing: e'eloze (exult, rejoice with leaping) and agil (rejoice with circling/spinning) — active, embodied joy of covenant worship. Elohei yish'i (God of my salvation) — yish'i is the possessive of yeshua' (salvation); the personal appropriation of the salvific God under conditions of total material deprivation. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:47: 'my spirit rejoices in God my Savior') echoes Habakkuk's declaration, applying the same structure to the incarnation as the ultimate form of YHWH's saving presence.
3.19: The Lord GOD is my strength; he gives me feet like a deer and makes me walk on the heights — ADONAI Elohim cheli: the covenant divine name with the additional Elohim (God-in-power) emphasizes both relational covenant (ADONAI) and sovereign might (Elohim) as the prophet's chayil (strength, army-force). Kachalot raglai (feet like hinds/deer) — the deer traverses mountain terrain inaccessible to human feet; 2 Sam 22:34 and Ps 18:33 use the same image for David's YHWH-empowered agility. Al bamotai yadrekheni (he makes me walk on my high places) — the mountain-heights are the covenant person's inheritance even when the valleys are stripped bare. The book ends with liturgical instruction ('for the choir director: on stringed instruments'), placing this theodicy-resolution within the community's ongoing worship.
